description:
An infrastructure engineering team keeps a plain-text list of all EC2 instance-type requests that arrive during the week.  
    Your job is to automate a tiny part of their provisioning workflow: summarise how many times each
    instance type appears in the list.

    Files and directories that already exist
    ----------------------------------------
    1. /home/user/provisioning/instance_requests.log  
       • A UTF-8 text file.  
       • Each line contains exactly one AWS instance-type string (e.g. “t2.micro”) and **nothing else**  
         – no leading / trailing whitespace.  
       • For this exercise the file already contains 7 lines (you do **not** create or
         modify this file).

    Required end-state
    ------------------
    1. Create a new directory  
       /home/user/provisioning/report
       if it does not already exist.
    2. Inside that directory create the file  
       /home/user/provisioning/report/instance_type_frequency.txt
    3. The file **must** contain five lines total:
       • Line-1 is a header, literally (case-sensitive):
         INSTANCE_TYPE COUNT
       • Lines-2..5 list every distinct instance type from the log together with
         the number of times it occurs.
       • Format for data lines:  
         <instance_type><single_space><integer_count>  
         (Exactly one ASCII space character between the two columns; no extra spaces.)
       • Sort order **strictly**:
         1. Descending by COUNT  
         2. For ties, ascending alphabetical order of INSTANCE_TYPE.
       • The file must end with a single newline.

    Example of the exact required content (header plus four data rows):
        INSTANCE_TYPE COUNT
        t2.micro 3
        m5.large 2
        c5.large 1
        c5.xlarge 1

    Constraints / hints
    -------------------
    • Achieve the result only with standard GNU/Linux tools already available in a minimal distribution:
      think along the lines of “sort”, “uniq -c”, “awk”, “printf”, etc.
    • You will probably need no more than two or three piped commands.
    • Do **not** hard-code the numbers; derive them from the log.
    • Make sure the script runs in /home/user (your default working directory) and that all paths you use are absolute or correctly relative.
    • The directory /home/user/provisioning is writable by you; you do **not** need root privileges.
    • When finished, you should be able to run  
          cat /home/user/provisioning/report/instance_type_frequency.txt  
      and see exactly the five lines shown above.

    Deliverable
    -----------
    Only the correctly populated /home/user/provisioning/report/instance_type_frequency.txt file is required; no extra scripts or temporary files are to be left behind.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing log file that the automated test will mount -->
    <file path="/home/user/provisioning/instance_requests.log"><![CDATA[
t2.micro
m5.large
t2.micro
c5.xlarge
t2.micro
m5.large
c5.large
]]></file>

    <!-- Expected file created by the agent -->
    <file path="/home/user/provisioning/report/instance_type_frequency.txt"><![CDATA[
INSTANCE_TYPE COUNT
t2.micro 3
m5.large 2
c5.large 1
c5.xlarge 1
]]></file>
